questions.question: Which option is a complete sentence?
questions.explanation: A sentence needs a complete thought with a subject and predicate.

questions.question: In "The artist painted a mural," what is the subject?
questions.explanation: The subject tells who or what the sentence is about.

questions.question: In "The artist painted a mural," what is the predicate?
questions.explanation: The predicate tells what the subject did.

questions.question: Which word is the verb in "The children laughed loudly"?
questions.explanation: Laughed is the action word.

questions.question: Which word is a noun in "Bright stars filled the sky"?
questions.explanation: Stars names things, so it is a noun.

questions.question: Which option best describes a sentence?
questions.explanation: The lesson explains sentences as complete thoughts.

questions.question: Which sentence has an object?
questions.explanation: The window receives the action opened.

questions.question: In "The dog chased the ball," what receives the action?
questions.explanation: The object receives the action of the verb.

questions.question: Which group contains a subject and a predicate?
questions.explanation: Birds is the subject and migrate is the predicate.

questions.question: Which word tells what the subject does in "Students study grammar"?
questions.explanation: Study is the verb in the predicate.

questions.question: Which option is a common noun?
questions.explanation: Teacher is a general name for a person.

questions.question: Which option is a proper noun?
questions.explanation: India names a specific country.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a verb as an action?
questions.explanation: Chopped is an action verb.

questions.question: In "Lena bought a notebook," what is the direct object?
questions.explanation: A notebook receives the action bought.

questions.question: Which word is the noun in "The quiet library closed early"?
questions.explanation: Library names a place.

questions.question: Which word is the verb in "The quiet library closed early"?
questions.explanation: Closed tells what the library did.

questions.question: Which option is an indirect object in "Ana gave Tom a pen"?
questions.explanation: Tom receives the direct object, a pen.

questions.question: Which question helps find the direct object?
questions.explanation: A direct object receives the verb action.

questions.question: Which sentence has no direct object?
questions.explanation: Slept does not transfer action to an object.

questions.question: Which option best identifies "homework" in "Ravi finished his homework"?
questions.explanation: Homework receives the action finished.

questions.question: Which word is an adjective in "The green door opened"?
questions.explanation: Green modifies the noun door.

questions.question: Which word is an adverb in "She answered quickly"?
questions.explanation: Quickly modifies the verb answered.

questions.question: Which word is a determiner in "Those books are heavy"?
questions.explanation: Those points to which books.

questions.question: Which sentence contains a prepositional phrase?
options: The keys are on the table.
questions.explanation: On the table is a prepositional phrase.

questions.question: Which word is the preposition in "We walked through the park"?
questions.explanation: Through shows the relationship between walked and park.

questions.question: Which word is a conjunction in "I studied and passed"?
questions.explanation: And connects studied and passed.

questions.question: Which conjunction best completes "I wanted tea, ___ I made coffee"?
questions.explanation: But shows contrast.

questions.question: Which option is a coordinating conjunction?
questions.explanation: Or connects choices.

questions.question: Which phrase modifies where the action happened in "They met after lunch"?
questions.explanation: After lunch is a prepositional phrase showing time.

questions.question: Which sentence correctly uses a connector?
options: She was tired, under she rested.
She was tired, so she rested.
She was tired, very she rested.
She was tired, the she rested.
questions.explanation: So connects cause and result.

questions.question: What person and number is "I"?
questions.explanation: I refers to the speaker alone.

questions.question: What person and number is "we"?
questions.explanation: We includes the speaker and at least one other person.

questions.question: What person is "you"?
questions.explanation: You addresses the listener or listeners.

questions.question: Which set is third person singular?
questions.explanation: He, she, and it refer to one subject being talked about.

questions.question: Which pronoun is third person plural?
questions.explanation: They refers to people or things being talked about.

questions.question: Which subject is third person singular?
questions.explanation: The cat is one thing being talked about.

questions.question: Which subject is first person plural?
questions.explanation: My brother and I includes the speaker.

questions.question: Which subject is second person plural?
questions.explanation: You all addresses multiple listeners.

questions.question: Which replacement is correct for "John and Mary"?
questions.explanation: John and Mary are third person plural.

questions.question: Which replacement is correct for "John and I"?
questions.explanation: John and I includes the speaker, so use we.

questions.question: Complete: "She ___ breakfast every morning."
questions.explanation: She is third person singular, so eat becomes eats.

questions.question: Complete: "They ___ to school."
questions.explanation: They is third person plural, so use the base form walk.

questions.question: Complete: "It ___ during winter."
questions.explanation: It is third person singular, so snow becomes snows.

questions.question: Complete: "We ___ on Saturdays."
questions.explanation: We is first person plural, so use the base form work.

questions.question: Complete: "You ___ to take a break."
questions.explanation: You uses the base form in simple present.

questions.question: Complete: "He ___ TV every evening."
questions.explanation: He is third person singular, and watch takes es.

questions.question: Complete: "They ___ eat meat."
questions.explanation: They takes do not in simple present negatives.

questions.question: Complete: "___ she like pizza?"
questions.explanation: She is third person singular, so the helper is does.

questions.question: Why is "Does she likes pizza?" wrong?
options: The main verb must stay base form after does
questions.explanation: Does is conjugated, so like stays base form.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Does goes before he, and play stays base form.

rounds.title: To Be and Real Use Challenge

questions.question: Complete: "I ___ a student."
questions.explanation: I takes am.

questions.question: Complete: "We ___ ready to go."
questions.explanation: We takes are.

questions.question: Complete: "Steve ___ playing outside."
questions.explanation: Steve is third person singular, so use is.

questions.question: Complete: "It ___ so cold yesterday."
questions.explanation: Yesterday marks past time; it takes was.

questions.question: Complete: "Someone called you when you ___ running outside."
questions.explanation: Past continuous with you uses were.

questions.question: Complete: "You must ___ kidding me!"
questions.explanation: After must, use base form be.

questions.question: Complete: "We have ___ here for ages."
questions.explanation: Have needs the past participle been.

questions.question: In "I have never regretted my choice," what does the verb phrase show?
questions.explanation: Have never regretted connects past experience to the present.

questions.question: Which sentence uses the correct form of to be?
questions.explanation: They takes are.

questions.question: What should you check first before choosing a verb form?
questions.explanation: Subject first, then person, number, and time.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: Liz is an accountant and she has a typical 8-5 job.
Liz are an accountant and she have a typical 8-5 job.
Liz is an accountant and she have a typical 8-5 job.
Liz are an accountant and she has a typical 8-5 job.
questions.explanation: Liz/she is third-person singular, so is and has are correct.

questions.question: Which subject takes the regular simple-present verb "works"?
questions.explanation: He is third-person singular, so a regular verb adds s.

questions.question: Complete: "We ___ every morning."
questions.explanation: We is plural, so the base form run is correct.

questions.question: Complete: "I ___ ready."
questions.explanation: The to be form with I in simple present is am.

questions.question: Complete: "They ___ ready."
questions.explanation: They takes are in simple present.

questions.question: What is the first step in subject-verb agreement?
options: Choose the verb that sounds fancy.
questions.explanation: The real subject controls the verb.

questions.question: Complete: "She ___ every day."
questions.explanation: She is third-person singular, so works is correct.

questions.question: Complete: "You ___ every morning."
questions.explanation: You uses the base verb in simple present.

questions.question: Which pair is correct for to be?
questions.explanation: He takes is; we takes are.

questions.question: Which sentence has correct agreement?
options: The subject and verb matches in person and number.
The subject and verb match in person and number.
The subjects and verb matches in person and number.
The subject and verbs matching in person and number.
questions.explanation: A compound subject takes the plural verb match.

questions.question: Complete: "It ___ outside in winter."
questions.explanation: It is third-person singular.

questions.question: Complete: "My friends ___ nearby."
questions.explanation: Friends is plural, so live is correct.

questions.question: Complete: "Everyone ___ going to the party."
questions.explanation: Everyone is singular.

questions.question: Complete: "Each of the students ___ a laptop."
questions.explanation: Each is singular.

questions.question: Complete: "John, as well as his friends, ___ soccer."
questions.explanation: As well as his friends is extra information; John controls the verb.

questions.question: Complete: "The book, as well as the notes, ___ on the table."
questions.explanation: Book is the real singular subject.

questions.question: Complete: "My friend, along with her colleagues, ___ at the door."
questions.explanation: Along with her colleagues does not change the singular subject friend.

questions.question: Complete: "No one ___ to work 16 hours a day."
questions.explanation: No one is a singular indefinite pronoun.

questions.question: Complete: "Someone ___ left their bag."
questions.explanation: Someone is singular, so has is correct.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: My sister, as well as her friends, love going to art galleries.
My sister, as well as her friends, are loving going to art galleries.
My sister, as well as her friends, have love going to art galleries.
My sister, as well as her friends, loves going to art galleries.
questions.explanation: The real subject is my sister.

questions.question: Complete: "A majority of the staff ___ in favor of the new policy."
questions.explanation: The lesson treats staff/majority as one unit in this example.

questions.question: Complete: "The team ___ preparing for the big game."
questions.explanation: Team is treated as one unit.

questions.question: Complete: "The family ___ going on vacation."
questions.explanation: Family is treated as a singular collective noun in American English.

questions.question: Complete: "The team ___ a lot of games."
questions.explanation: Team is singular here, so loses is correct.

questions.question: Complete: "Neither the cat nor the dogs ___ around the house."
questions.explanation: Dogs is closer to the verb and plural.

questions.question: Complete: "Either my teammates or the captain ___ the warm-up drills."
questions.explanation: Captain is closer and singular.

questions.question: Complete: "Neither the teacher nor the students ___ ready."
questions.explanation: Students is closer and plural.

questions.question: Complete: "Neither the students nor the teacher ___ ready."
questions.explanation: Teacher is closer and singular.

questions.question: Complete: "There ___ many reasons for the delay."
questions.explanation: The real subject is many reasons, plural.

questions.question: Complete: "There ___ a book and two pens on the table."
questions.explanation: In the shown pattern, the verb agrees with the closest subject after there: a book.

questions.question: Complete: "There ___ two pens and a book on the table."
questions.explanation: The closest subject after there is two pens, plural.

questions.question: Complete: "A lot of the cake ___ gone."
questions.explanation: Cake is singular/uncountable, so is is correct.

questions.question: Complete: "A lot of the cakes ___ gone."
questions.explanation: Cakes is plural, so are is correct.

questions.question: Complete: "Some of the money ___ missing."
questions.explanation: Money is singular/uncountable.

questions.question: Complete: "Some of the people ___ missing."
questions.explanation: People is plural.

questions.question: Complete: "Ten dollars ___ a high price to pay for socks."
questions.explanation: Ten dollars is treated as one amount here.
